examples.title: Distance Vector Routing Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for Bellman-Ford based distance vector routing
examples.code:
INITIALIZE ROUTING TABLE
BEGIN
 FOR each destination D in network DO
 IF D is directly connected THEN
 distance[D] = cost to D
 nextHop[D] = D
 ELSE
 distance[D] = INFINITY
 nextHop[D] = NULL
 END IF
 END FOR

UPDATE ROUTING TABLE
BEGIN
 FOR each received update from neighbor N DO
 FOR each destination D DO
 newDist = cost to N + distance[N][D]
 IF newDist < distance[D] THEN
 distance[D] = newDist
 nextHop[D] = N
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
END
examples.explanation: Each router maintains a table of shortest distances to all destinations. Periodically exchanges this table with directly connected neighbors. Updates its table if a shorter path is found through a neighbor using the Bellman-Ford equation.

examples.title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for link state routing using Dijkstra's algorithm
examples.code:
DIJKSTRA'S ALGORITHM
BEGIN
 SET S = {source} // Set of visited vertices
 FOR each vertex v DO
 distance[v] = INFINITY
 previous[v] = NULL
 END FOR
 distance[source] = 0

 WHILE S does not contain all vertices DO
 SELECT vertex u in V-S with minimum distance
 ADD u to S
 FOR each neighbor v of u DO
 alt = distance[u] + cost(u,v)
 IF alt < distance[v] THEN
 distance[v] = alt
 previous[v] = u
 END IF
 END FOR
 END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: Link state protocols like OSPF use this algorithm. Each router has complete topology map. Calculates shortest path to all destinations by progressively building set of visited nodes with known shortest distances.
examples.timeComplexity: O(V²) basic, O(E log V) with priority queue

examples.title: Packet Forwarding Process
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how router forwards packets based on routing table
examples.code:
PACKET FORWARDING
BEGIN
 WHEN packet arrives at router input DO
 Read destination IP from packet header
 Extract destination network prefix
 
 // Longest Prefix Match
 FOR each entry in routing table DO
 IF (destination IP AND subnet_mask) == network_address THEN
 candidate = entry
 END IF
 END FOR
 SELECT entry with longest prefix match
 
 IF entry found THEN
 Decrement TTL by 1
 Recalculate header checksum
 Forward to nextHop interface
 ELSE
 Drop packet
 Send ICMP Destination Unreachable
 END IF
 END WHEN
END
examples.explanation: Router performs longest prefix match to find best route. Decrements TTL and updates checksum before forwarding. If no matching route exists, packet is dropped and error is reported.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) with TCAM, O(log n) with binary search on prefixes

examples.title: IP Packet Fragmentation Process
examples.description: Pseudocode showing packet fragmentation at router when MTU exceeded
examples.code:
FRAGMENTATION
BEGIN
 WHEN packet arrives with size > outgoing_MTU DO
 original_ID = packet.identification
 max_payload = outgoing_MTU - 20 // minus IP header
 offset = 0
 
 WHILE remaining_data > max_payload DO
 Create new fragment
 fragment.identification = original_ID
 fragment.MF = 1 // More Fragments flag
 fragment.offset = offset / 8 // in 8-byte units
 fragment.header = copy original header
 fragment.data = max_payload bytes from original
 Send fragment
 offset = offset + max_payload
 remaining_data = remaining_data - max_payload
 END WHILE
 
 // Last fragment
 Create final fragment
 fragment.identification = original_ID
 fragment.MF = 0 // No more fragments
 fragment.offset = offset / 8
 fragment.header = copy original header
 fragment.data = remaining_data
 Send fragment
 END WHEN
END
examples.explanation: When a packet exceeds outgoing interface MTU, router fragments it. Each fragment carries same identification, MF flag set on all but last fragment, and offset indicating position in original packet.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of fragments
